classification.summary: This act allows the presiding judge of the Fayette County Court to appoint two justices of the peace to hold court if the presiding judge is absent, unable to attend, or declines to act in a specific case. These appointed justices would be sworn in and exercise the powers of the presiding judge, receiving the same compensation.

It outlines a procedural mechanism for ensuring the court can function in the absence or recusal of the presiding judge. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT providing for Filling Vacancies in the Office of Public Binder
classification.summary: This act outlines the procedures for filling vacancies in the office of Public Binder. If a vacancy occurs during a legislative session, it is to be filled by a joint vote of the two Houses. If the vacancy occurs when the legislature is not in session, the Governor makes an appointment until the next legislative session, at which point the General Assembly will elect someone for the remainder of the term. It also addresses a specific existing vacancy due to resignation.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the process for filling vacancies in the office of Public Binder. It specifies how these vacancies are to be filled by either the General Assembly or the Governor, depending on whether the legislature is in session. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the navigability of a creek and has no apparent connection to racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 12 of the General Statutes, Concerning the Caldwell Court of Common Pleas
classification.summary: This act details the operational procedures for the Caldwell Court of Common Pleas, including jury summoning, the duties and compensation of county officials (sheriff, jailer, coroner, constables, marshals), and the responsibilities and fees of the clerk. It specifies that the clerk of the Caldwell Circuit Court will also serve as the clerk for the Court of Common Pleas, and that records from this court will have the same legal force as those from circuit courts. The act also mandates the procurement of record books for the new court, to be paid for by the Public Treasury.

It outlines the roles of various legal officials, jury selection, and record-keeping. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that suggest differential treatment based on race. The language used is purely procedural and administrative. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act prohibits livestock from running at large on public roads in Clark and Montgomery Counties. Violators face fines, with penalties increasing for subsequent offenses. The fines are to be paid into the county jury fund. The act takes effect on June 1, 1871, and judges are to instruct grand juries about it.

classification.title: AN ACT to repeal an act, entitled "An act for the benefit of the Negroes and Mulattoes of this Commonwealth," approved March 9, 1867.
classification.summary: This act repeals a previous act that provided benefits to Negroes and Mulattoes. It then states that Negroes and Mulattoes will be taxed the same per capita and on real and personal estate as the white population, with no additional taxes. This effectively removes any special provisions or benefits previously afforded to Black individuals and places them under the same tax structure as white citizens, while explicitly mentioning racial groups.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'Negroes and Mulattoes' and repeals an act that was 'for the benefit' of these groups. While it states that they will be taxed the same as the white population, the act's purpose is to remove prior beneficial legislation and establish a uniform tax structure that still explicitly differentiates based on race. This is a form of racial regulation, characteristic of Jim Crow era legislation, even if it appears to equalize taxation in one aspect. The explicit mention of racial groups and the repeal of a beneficial act for them strongly indicate a Jim Crow context. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language and the nature of the repeal.

It also outlines provisions for the payment of pro tempore judges and chancellors, to be deducted from the increased salary of the judge or chancellor of the district where the allowance is made. An exception is made for the judge of the court of common pleas for Warren county, whose salary will not be increased beyond the amount already fixed by law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 47, Criminal Code of Practice.
classification.summary: This act amends section 47 of the Criminal Code of Practice. It allows a defendant to request a change of judge or magistrate if they believe they cannot secure a fair trial before the current judicial officer. If an affidavit is filed, the judge or magistrate must retire, and another shall take their place. Once a judge has retired and been replaced, no further affidavits can be filed by the same party against the new judicial officer.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the process for recusal of judges or magistrates in criminal proceedings. It provides a mechanism for defendants to request a change of venue if they believe they cannot receive a fair trial. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high as the text is clear and unambiguous regarding its purpose.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to provide for the improvement of the navigation of Licking river, and for other purposes," approved March 11, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the improvement of Licking River. It details the appointment of overseers, the allocation of labor from male inhabitants nearest to the stream, and exemptions for those working on the stream from road work. It also specifies penalties for failing to work, outlines penalties for presiding judges who fail to carry out the provisions, exempts certain months from river work, and sets penalties for obstructing the river. County judges are to appoint receivers for funds or property arising from the act.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the improvement of navigation on Licking River and the organization of labor for this purpose. It details the appointment of overseers, the selection of laborers from the local male population, and penalties for non-compliance. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is on labor organization for public works and river navigation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1871'.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Common Schools in Kentucky
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Superintendent of Public Instruction to certify to the Auditor and direct payment for common schools in specific districts. Payments are to be made from surplus funds for the school year ending June 30, 1871, or from the surplus bond fund, at a rate not exceeding previous payments. Conditions include ensuring payment hasn't already been made, prorating for schools taught less than the legal time, and requiring reports to be submitted by July 1, 1871.

classification.title: AN ACT to require Railroad Companies to keep open their ticket offices for one hour immediately next before the departure of their trains.
classification.summary: This act mandates that all railroad companies in Kentucky must keep their ticket offices open for one hour immediately preceding the departure of their trains. Failure to comply will result in a fine of not less than ten nor more than fifty dollars, recoverable before a justice of the peace or police judge. Additionally, the company will be liable for damages to any aggrieved person.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to give bond, deposit money in bank, and liabilities thereunder.
classification.summary: This act outlines the requirements for a court-appointed receiver, including taking an oath, posting a bond of $200,000, and depositing all funds received into a Louisville-based bank with a capital of at least $100,000. The bank must also provide a bond of $100,000. The receiver is required to report on the aggregate funds twice a year and publish a list of unclaimed funds older than two years in Louisville daily papers. The receiver is compensated at a rate of ten cents per publication for each sum and name listed.

It specifies bond amounts, banking requirements, and reporting procedures for court funds. classification.summary: This act amends the Civil Code of Practice concerning Forcible Entry and Detainer actions. It allows courts to require a new bond from the traversor if the original bond is insufficient to cover potential damages, costs, and expenses. Failure to provide a new bond will result in the dismissal of the traverse. It also provides a mechanism for sureties on these bonds to be released by requiring the traversor to execute a new bond, with their liability for prior damages remaining intact.

classification.title: AN ACT authorizing and directing the sale of the interest and stock owned by the State of Kentucky in Turnpike Roads or Turnpike Road Companies.
classification.summary: This act authorizes and directs the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund to sell the State's stock in turnpike roads or companies. Sales are to be made at the discretion of the Commissioners to secure the highest price and best promote the State's interest. Preference is given to county courts, but if they fail to purchase, the stock can be sold to other persons or corporations on a credit of not more than five years. Obligations for deferred payments are to be made payable to the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund and bear 6% interest.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the sale of state-owned stock in turnpike road companies. It outlines the process and terms for these sales, prioritizing county courts but allowing sales to other entities. There is no mention of race, and the subject matter (infrastructure finance) does not inherently suggest a racial dimension. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend chapter84,titleRoads and Passways,RevisedStatutes.
classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 84 of the Revised Statutes concerning Roads and Passways. It allows county courts to establish passways over the land of others for owners of iron works or iron mines to access ore or wood for coaling purposes. It also allows owners of wood and timberlands adjacent to railroads or navigable streams to apply for passways to haul wood, etc. Damages for these passways are to be assessed and paid by the applicant.

Process will be returnable to the Tuesday after the third Monday of each of these months.

The first act (sections 4-6) deals with outstanding debts and appropriations for completing offices, and payments for services rendered. The second act (Chapter 1464) specifically changes the time of holding the Montgomery Quarterly Court. The third act (Chapter 1479) directs the Governor to appoint commissioners to revise, digest, and compile the Statute Laws and Codes of Practice of Kentucky. None of these acts, including the one concerning the Montgomery Quarterly Court, contain any provisions related to racial segregation, discrimination, or any other characteristic of Jim Crow laws. classification.title: An Act to amend the Common School Laws of Kentucky
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for establishing and maintaining common school houses in Kentucky districts. It details requirements for school house construction, the duties of school trustees in levying taxes for materials and labor, the collection of these taxes by the sheriff, and penalties for failure to comply with the provisions. It also specifies a deadline for the Eighth and Ninth Congressional Districts to have a good school house by November 1, 1872, with potential indictment and fines for non-compliance.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the administrative and logistical aspects of establishing and maintaining common school houses. It details the responsibilities of school officials, the methods of funding construction and repairs, and the penalties for non-compliance. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1871.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the erection of a Warehouse for the Kentucky Penitentiary, and to appropriate funds for its erection and completion.
classification.summary: This act appropriates $28,311 for the construction of a warehouse block at the Kentucky Penitentiary. It appoints the Governor, Auditor, and Treasurer as a commission to oversee the project, including hiring an architect, soliciting bids, and awarding contracts to the lowest and best bidder. The commission is authorized to issue certificates for work completed, and the Auditor will draw warrants for payment. The keeper of the Penitentiary will pay 6% annual interest on the invested money after completion.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the appropriation of funds and the process for constructing a warehouse for the Kentucky Penitentiary. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about infrastructure and financial management, with no racial component.

classification.summary: This act details the financial management and compensation for county commissioners and teachers within the common school system. It outlines how funds are allocated, how commissioners are paid, and how compensation is apportioned when commissioners change during a school year. It also specifies the conditions under which school districts are entitled to their proportion of the school fund for paying teachers.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Article Four of Chapter Eighty-Three of the General Statutes, Relating to Common Schools
classification.summary: This act amends sections of the General Statutes concerning common schools. It modifies the reporting schedule for school commissioners and the conditions under which school districts are entitled to state revenue. Specifically, it changes the reporting months from May, July, and February to January, April, and July, and clarifies the payment percentages for schools kept for only half a session. It also outlines the process for reporting school sessions and receiving payments, including provisions for cities organized as single districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administration and funding of common schools in Kentucky. It details the procedural requirements for school commissioners and district trustees regarding reporting school sessions and receiving state funds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely administrative and financial, related to the operational aspects of the public education system at the time. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act repealing in part the act declaring the Rolling Fork of Salt River a Navigable Stream
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act from 1870 that partially repealed the declaration of the Rolling Fork of Salt River as a navigable stream. The amendment changes the point above which the river is no longer considered navigable from 'above the point at which the Bards-town and Green River Turnpike Road crosses the same' to 'above the mouth of the Beech Fork'. The act also includes a provision that it will only be binding on the citizens of New Haven if a majority of voters in an election approve of repealing the law declaring the Rolling Fork a navigable stream, with the stated view of erecting a mill at New Haven.

classification.title: AN ACT to change the Boundary Lines between the Counties of Ohio and Butler
classification.summary: This act adjusts the boundary between Ohio and Butler counties in Kentucky. A portion of Ohio County, known as 'Little Bend' and situated in a bend of Green River, is transferred to Butler County. This transferred area will be part of magistrates' district No. 1, and voters within this new boundary will vote at the Morgantown precinct. Surveyors from both counties are appointed to define and mark the new boundary line, with compensation to be paid by Butler County.

classification.summary: This act grants incorporated banks in Kentucky the power to purchase bills of exchange or negotiable notes payable at their own banks or other banks in the same town or city. It also gives them the right to receive articles of value, bills of exchange, or promissory notes as pledges or security, and the authority to sell these pledges to pay debts. It explicitly states that this act does not confer power to sell real estate except through judicial proceedings.

classification.title: AN ACT to change the time for the payment of the Revenue into the State Treasury,and to amend the Revenue Laws.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky's revenue laws by changing the payment schedule for sheriffs to the State Treasury. Sheriffs are now required to report and pay collected taxes to the Auditor of Public Accounts on the first day of October and every sixty days thereafter. They must fully account for and pay all taxes by April 1st of the following year. A penalty of five percent additional tax is imposed on individuals failing to pay their taxes by April 1st of the year following the assessment. The act also changes the timing for sheriffs to produce a quietus from the Auditor to the county court.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the collection and payment of state revenue by sheriffs. It outlines deadlines for reporting and remitting taxes, introduces a penalty for late payment, and adjusts administrative procedures for tax settlements. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to establish a State House of Reform for Juvenile Delinquents," approved February 15th, 1869.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law establishing a State House of Reform for juvenile delinquents. It mandates that all white male and female persons between the ages of eight and seventeen, convicted of vagrancy, petit larceny, or any crime less than murder, shall be committed to this House of Reform. It also specifies that females will not be committed until the Governor announces that provisions have been made for their care in the institution.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that 'all white male and female persons' are to be committed to the House of Reform. This explicit racial classification, even if seemingly inclusive of all races, is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation which often established separate or differential treatment based on race, or in this case, defined the scope of application of a reformatory for a specific racial group. The focus on 'white' persons suggests a potential for differential treatment or exclusion of non-white individuals from this specific reformatory, or at least a deliberate racial categorization in the law. Therefore, it is classified as Jim Crow. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of race. The category is incarceration as it deals with commitment to a reformatory. The document type is session laws as it is an act passed by the General Assembly.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize Life Insurance Companies to make special deposits of securities in the Insurance Department.
classification.summary: This act allows life insurance companies incorporated in Kentucky to make special deposits of mortgages on unencumbered real estate in the state with the Insurance Department. These deposits, amounting to at least $20,000, are to be held in trust for the benefit of holders of registered policies and annuity bonds. The act outlines the procedures for transferring these mortgages and issuing registered policies or annuity bonds in exchange.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns financial regulations for life insurance companies, specifically regarding the deposit of securities. It does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly financial regulation and has no apparent racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to regulate the business of life insurance," approved March 10, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends existing legislation concerning life insurance companies. It details requirements for the registration of policies and annuity bonds, including the deposit of real estate mortgages as security. The Commissioner of Insurance is tasked with valuing these policies and bonds, and companies must provide regular reports on their condition and premium accounts. The act also specifies that the state is not obligated to pay these policies.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the regulation of life insurance companies, specifically focusing on the registration of policies and annuity bonds, the deposit of securities (real estate mortgages), and the reporting requirements for these companies to the Commissioner of Insurance. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1871, indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to regulate the deposit of mortgages by life insurance companies" approved March 12, 1870.
classification.summary: This act details the procedures for life insurance companies to deposit mortgages with the State Treasurer and Commissioner of Insurance. It outlines requirements for registration, withdrawal of excess mortgages, and the handling of policy and annuity bonds. The act specifies the salary of the Treasurer for these services and how expenses are to be paid by the companies.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1871. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about financial and administrative regulations for insurance companies and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to regulate the business of life insurance" approved March 11, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends existing legislation concerning life insurance companies. It outlines procedures for the Commissioner of Insurance to handle company finances, policy issues, and potential insolvency. Specifically, it details how the Commissioner should report companies in a condition that makes issuing new policies injurious to the public interest to the Attorney General, who would then seek a court order to close the company and appoint a receiver. The receiver would then investigate the company's financial health and report to the court.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the regulation of life insurance companies, including procedures for financial oversight, policy issuance, and receivership in cases of insolvency. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header '98 LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 1871 .'.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the winding up of the affairs of insolvent insurance companies, and for the protection of the policy and annuity holders of such companies," approved March 12, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the winding up of insolvent insurance companies. It outlines procedures for receivers, including notifying policyholders, potentially reinsuring policies, and distributing assets. The focus is on the financial and administrative aspects of company dissolution and the protection of policyholders' interests.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the process for handling insolvent insurance companies in Kentucky in 1871. It outlines the duties of a receiver, the notification of policyholders, and the distribution of assets. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute is purely financial and administrative in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to regulate the business of insurance,' approved March 19, 1870.
classification.summary: This act details the procedures for receivers of insurance companies when their assets exceed their liabilities. It outlines how excess funds should be invested and managed, and how deficiencies should be addressed by converting assets with the consent of the Treasurer. It also mandates an annual investigation by an actuary to assess the company's financial health and the disposition of any surplus assets, including setting aside a portion for a contingent fund and issuing scrip to stockholders.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial regulation and management of insurance companies, specifically the role of receivers and the handling of surplus or insufficient funds. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an amendment to a previous act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the settlement of the affairs of insolvent insurance companies."
classification.summary: This act details the powers and responsibilities of a receiver appointed to manage the affairs of insolvent insurance companies. It outlines how surplus funds should be distributed, including provisions for scrip issuance, interest payments, and redemption. It also grants the receiver authority to compromise claims, purchase policies, and employ clerks and actuaries, with their compensation subject to the approval of the Commissioner of the Insurance Department. The compensation for the receiver and staff is to be paid from the company's funds.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the process of liquidating insolvent insurance companies and distributing their assets. It details the role of a receiver, the issuance of scrip to policyholders, and the distribution of remaining surplus funds. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1871, indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: RESOLUTION instructingour Senatorsand requestingour Representativesin Congress to oppose the passage of the CincinnatiSouthern Railway Bill
classification.summary: This resolution from the Kentucky General Assembly instructs Kentucky's Senators and requests its Representatives in Congress to oppose the passage of the Cincinnati Southern Railway Bill. The bill, pending in the U.S. Senate, would authorize the city of Cincinnati to construct a railway across Kentucky without the consent of the Kentucky Legislature. The Assembly argues this would be an unwarranted interference by the Federal Government with the state's territory and jurisdiction, violating the U.S. Constitution and fundamental governmental principles. Failure to protest would be construed as tacit approval.

It also clarifies that the act does not prevent the issuance and levy of attachments or sustaining actions against the corporation as a foreign corporation.

classification.title: An Act for the relief of Dennis Mulligan
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Auditor to draw a warrant on the Treasurer for $517.75 in favor of Dennis Mulligan. This payment is to compensate Mulligan for the purchase money and interest he paid for a house and lot, which he subsequently lost due to a legal decision in favor of Martha Ann Corbin and her daughter, Martha Ann Corbin. The Corbins' claim to the property was based on an act of the General Assembly from April 4, 1861, which vested rights in them that had come to the Commonwealth by escheat or otherwise. The statute acknowledges that the Commonwealth is responsible to Mulligan because he bought the property in good faith under the Commonwealth and lost it due to the Corbins' claim, which originated from the Commonwealth's acquisition of the property.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns a property dispute and reimbursement of funds. It details a legal battle over a house and lot where Dennis Mulligan purchased the property from the Commonwealth, but later lost it due to a claim by the Corbin family, which was based on an earlier legislative act. The Commonwealth, having guaranteed the title, is deemed responsible for Mulligan's losses. The act is a financial remedy for Mulligan. classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties and powers of road overseers in Kentucky for the year 1871. It details procedures for collecting road taxes and fines, requiring individuals to work on public roads, and setting penalties for failure to comply. It also specifies when hands can be called out to work, notice requirements, and compensation for teams and wagons used in road maintenance. The statute focuses on the administration and enforcement of labor and tax obligations for road upkeep.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details regulations concerning public road maintenance, including the collection of taxes and fines, and the requirement for individuals to perform labor. It outlines the responsibilities of road overseers and the procedures for enforcing these obligations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general law governing public works and labor. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. The category is 'labor' as it directly pertains to mandatory work and its enforcement. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1871'.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to roads and turnpikes
classification.summary: This act details the duties of road overseers, including prosecuting individuals who obstruct public roads, erecting road signs, and keeping accounts of funds. It also outlines penalties for obstruction and the responsibilities of ferry owners. Overseers are liable to presentment and fines, and are required to work out personal service or taxes for road maintenance. The act specifies that at least half of the hands in a district must work on roads at any given time, unless removing special obstructions.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the administration and maintenance of public roads, including penalties for obstruction and the duties of road overseers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 1871'.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts concerning roads in this Commonwealth.
classification.summary: This act details the management and funding of public roads in Kentucky. It outlines penalties for road commissioners or overseers who embezzle road funds, specifies the road district of Greenupsburg and the control of its town authorities over roads, streets, and alleys, and mandates the publication of road laws. It also repeals former special road laws for Greenup County and establishes the process for opening, closing, or changing roads by order of the county court. The second part of the provided text is a separate act authorizing the County Judge of Ohio County to sign certain orders and legalize them, related to land purchases.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text primarily deals with the administration, funding, and regulation of public roads and road taxes. It also includes a separate act concerning land orders in Ohio County. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The laws appear to be general in nature, applying to all citizens regarding road management and land transactions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT concerning the PoliceCourt of the Town of Winchester, Clark County
classification.summary: This act repeals a provision from a 1867 act concerning the town of Winchester's Police Court. Specifically, it removes the requirement that the town be liable for officers' costs and fees if a defendant is acquitted.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains three separate acts from the 1871 Laws of Kentucky. The first act authorizes a judge to sign orders related to appropriated land in Ohio County. The second act amends the charter of Cave Hill Cemetery Company to allow for the extension of Broadway street. The third act, concerning the Police Court of Winchester, Clark County, repeals a section that made the town liable for officers' costs and fees in cases of acquittal. None of these acts contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation, which are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. The document type is 'session_laws' as these are individual acts passed during a legislative session. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorizethe County Court of Knox County to issuebonds to raisemoney to erect a Court-house,and other public buildings,in said County .
classification.summary: This act authorizes the County Court of Knox County to issue bonds, not exceeding twelve thousand five hundred dollars, to raise money for the construction and furnishing of a court-house and other public buildings. The bonds are to be repaid within ten years with an annual interest of up to ten percent, and ten percent of the principal annually. A tax not exceeding twenty cents per one hundred dollars of taxable property is to be levied for this purpose.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to authorize the Fulton County Court to levy and collect a tax in said County to repair the Court-house in said County," approved January 26, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act from January 26, 1870, to allow the Fulton County Court to levy and collect taxes not only for repairing the courthouse but also for repairing or rebuilding the county jail. The act specifies that this amendment is to be added to the end of section one of the original act.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct legislative acts from 1871. The first act (sections 3-6) deals with enabling a court to pay bonds and interest by increasing county levies and ad valorem taxes, and potentially applying these funds to courthouse and public building construction. The second act (Chapter 1147) amends a prior act to allow Fulton County Court to levy taxes for repairing or rebuilding the county jail, in addition to the courthouse. Neither of these acts explicitly or implicitly mentions race or racial segregation. They are purely financial and administrative measures related to public infrastructure. Therefore, they are not Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Barbourville
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of Barbourville broad powers to manage town affairs, including opening streets, selling town property, directing street improvements, levying ad valorem and poll taxes, taxing auction sales and exhibitions, and licensing various businesses such as taverns, groceries, and places of public resort within the town or within one mile of its limits. It also empowers them to suppress tippling and gambling houses and to fine violators of their ordinances, with specific penalties for riots, unlawful assemblies, breaches of the peace, and disturbing religious worship. The revenue from licenses and fines is to be used for the benefit of the town.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and fiscal powers granted to the board of trustees of the town of Barbourville. It covers aspects like property management, taxation, licensing of businesses, and enforcement of local ordinances. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted are general municipal powers applicable to all citizens within the town's jurisdiction. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 100 of the General Statutes, Title 'Towns,' So as to Apply to the Town of Vanceburg.
classification.summary: This statute grants the board of trustees of Vanceburg discretionary power to issue licenses for the sale of liquors, tax various public establishments, and suspend licenses for unlawful gaming, disorderly conduct, or intoxication. It also allows the board to close establishments selling liquor for up to 24 hours if public peace requires it. Additionally, the board is empowered to suppress tippling-houses, bawdy-houses, houses of ill-fame, and regulate public order and animal control within the town.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of liquor sales, public establishments, and general town order. While it grants broad powers to a local board, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race or racial segregation. The regulations apply to all individuals and establishments within the town, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in 1871. The confidence is high because the text clearly lacks any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Mississippi and Southern Atlantic Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act grants a charter to the Mississippi and Southern Atlantic Railroad Company, outlining its powers and responsibilities. These include the ability to condemn land, be taxed at a rate consistent with other railroads, purchase and hold other railroads, sell or lease its own, build branches, consolidate with other companies, and connect with other railroads via various means. It also details provisions for borrowing money, securing repayment through mortgages, and establishes the enforceability of its contracts. A key provision reserves the right of the General Assembly to regulate freight and passenger transportation charges. The act takes effect upon its passage.

Tavern keepers will only be granted the privilege of selling by the drink, with penalties for selling more than one drink at a time to a person. classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 169
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and duties of the common council of the city of Paducah. It details their authority to pass resolutions for financial matters exceeding twenty thousand dollars, execute bonds for renewing existing liabilities, make provisions for debt payment, levy taxes, pledge or sell city property, and make regulations for voter elections. It also grants them the power to accept, lay out, open, widen, or extend streets, alleys, market-places, wharves, or other public landings, including the condemnation of private property for public use.

It also includes a provision limiting the cost of improvements to half the value of the property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Specifically, it states that no person shall be eligible unless they are a 'white male citizen' of the city, at least twenty-five years of age, a licensed practicing lawyer for five years preceding election, and a resident of the city for at least one year preceding election. The statute also details the jurisdiction and powers of the Paducah city court.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that a judge of the city court must be a 'white male citizen'. This is a clear indicator of racial discrimination and a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation, which often restricted political and judicial offices to white individuals. Therefore, the statute is classified as Jim Crow. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial qualifier. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the context 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year 1871. The category is 'other' as it pertains to qualifications for public office rather than a specific area like education or housing.

classification.summary: This statute details the procedures for fines and penalties within the city court of Paducah, Kentucky. It outlines how fines are collected, paid to the city treasurer, and the rights of defendants to replevy judgments. It also specifies how judgments can be enforced through attachments and the option for defendants to work off fines and costs in the city workhouse or on public works.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Paducah
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties of the city assessor in Paducah, Kentucky, for the year 1871. It details the process of assessing personal property, real estate, and a poll tax. Notably, it requires the assessor to note "colored persons" on his book when assessing the poll tax, indicating a separate or distinct categorization for this group in the tax assessment process. The statute also describes the procedure for creating and filing assessment books as public records and the establishment of tax rates by the common council.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored persons' in the context of poll tax assessment. While it doesn't establish separate facilities or explicitly restrict rights in the way typical Jim Crow laws do, the explicit categorization of 'colored persons' for taxation purposes, especially in the post-Reconstruction era, suggests a potential for discriminatory application or at least a racialized administrative practice. However, the primary function of the statute is tax assessment and administration, not direct racial segregation or disenfranchisement. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, but the explicit mention of race warrants a high confidence score and the identification of 'colored persons' as an explicit racial indicator. The category is null because the statute is not directly about education, housing, marriage, public accommodations, incarceration, voting, or labor, but rather about taxation and administrative procedures.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Paducah
classification.summary: This act details the process for appointing supervisors of taxes in Paducah, outlining their duties in reviewing and correcting tax assessments made by the assessor. It specifies procedures for valuing property, summoning owners for valuation changes, compelling witness attendance, keeping records, reporting to the common council, and the council's power to hear complaints and adjust tax lists. It also establishes that there will be one assessor and one collector for Paducah's taxable property.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the administrative and procedural aspects of tax assessment and collection within the city of Paducah. It outlines the roles and responsibilities of tax supervisors, assessors, and the common council in managing property valuations and tax lists. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Paducah
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Paducah, Kentucky. Section 148 grants the common council the power to levy a tax for common schools, but explicitly states that this tax shall not be levied upon the property of colored citizens, nor shall colored citizens participate in the common schools. Section 149 details how tax levies for bonds and schools should be designated.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that taxes for common schools shall not be levied upon the property of 'colored citizens' and that these citizens shall not participate in the common schools. This is a clear instance of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is from the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' for the year 1871. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. Human review is not needed as the statute is clear.

This is a clear racial restriction on holding public office.

It specifies the order of levying, advertising requirements, sale procedures, and the fees associated with these actions. The act also mandates specific documentation and record-keeping for tax levies and sales.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Paducah, and to Authorize the City to Levy and Collect Taxes, and for Other Purposes.
classification.summary: This act details the process for the city of Paducah to collect unpaid taxes. It outlines procedures for instituting equity proceedings, attachment, and garnishment against delinquent taxpayers. It also specifies that only one bed and bedding, and the wearing apparel of the taxpayer, are exempt from levy and sale for these taxes. Furthermore, it establishes that sales of property after January 10th do not defeat the city's lien for taxes, and the title acquired by a purchaser under a collector's sale relates back to January 10th of the assessment year. Finally, it outlines the appointment and qualifications for the City Treasurer, requiring the treasurer to be a white male citizen over twenty-four years old and a resident of Paducah for at least one year.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the City Treasurer must be a 'white male citizen'. This is a clear racial and gender-based qualification, which is characteristic of Jim Crow era legislation. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial requirement. The category is 'other' as it pertains to municipal administration and taxation rather than a specific area like education or housing. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header '192 LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 1871 .'. The racial indicator is 'explicit' because the term 'white male' is used.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties, responsibilities, and appointment procedures for the city treasurer of Paducah. It details how the treasurer should handle city funds, keep accounts, report financial statements to the common council, and the conditions for reappointment and bond execution. It also specifies the compensation and lien rights related to the treasurer's office.

It focuses on financial management, reporting, and the appointment process for the office. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1871'.

classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the qualifications for common school trustees in Paducah, stating they must have the same qualifications as councilmen as to 'age, sex, color, and property.' While 'color' is mentioned, the statute does not explicitly mandate segregation or discrimination based on race. However, the inclusion of 'color' as a qualification, in the context of the Jim Crow era, strongly suggests an implicit racial component in the selection process, potentially for maintaining segregation in schools or limiting the participation of Black individuals. Despite this implicit racial element, the statute itself does not enact discriminatory practices like separate facilities or unequal treatment, which are hallmarks of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for being a Jim Crow law, but with a high confidence due to the implicit racial indicator. classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Paducah
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Paducah, clarifying the validity of existing records and contracts, allowing for late appointments of officers by the common council, and establishing the effective date of the new charter. It also specifies that current officers and city council members will continue in their roles until their successors are elected and qualified under the new charter, and that recent redistricting will not vacate any council offices. The act repeals the previous incorporation act of March 10th, 1856, and its amendments.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the administrative and governmental structure of the city of Paducah. It addresses the validity of records, the appointment of officers, the effective date of a new charter, and the continuation of existing officials in their roles. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 1267 .
AN ACT providingfor the appointment of a County Treasurerfor Lincoln
County .
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. The judge of the county court of Lincoln county
shall have the power ,and it is made his duty ,to appoint
a county treasurerforsaidcounty,who shallhold his office
during the continuance in officeof said county judge ,and
who shall receive,and under the orders of the said court
disburse, allfunds belonging to,and under the controlof,
said court,to whom said court shall allow a reasonable
compensation forhis services.
§2 . The court shall requireof the person so appointed
to the officeof county treasurer,before he shall receive
any money under his said appointment ,or proceed to the
execution of the duties of his said office,to execute a cov-
enant ,payable to the county judge of said county ,for the
use and benefit of the county court of Lincoln county ,
with one or more sureties,to be approved by thecourt,
conditioned for the faithfulperformance of his duties as
county treasurerof said county ; and that he will faith-
fullyaccount for and pay over all moneys at the time ,
and to the personor personsallowedthe same ,under the
order of said court,upon which covenant ,on the order of
said court, suitmay be brought and recoveryhad in a
court of competent jurisdictionupon a breach thereof.
The said county treasurer shall also,at the same time ,
and before said court,take an oath,or affirm that he will
faithfullydischarge the duties of hissaid office.
§ 3. The county judge shallhave the power to make , or
cause to be made ,a settlementwith the present treasurer
of said county ,and with any and allpersons having funds
in theirhands going or belonging to said county court,or
under itscontrol,which settlement,when made ,shall be
returned to said court,and when approved by the court,
shallbe recorded; and thereupon the court may order
payment thereof to the county treasurer, whose receipt
for the same shallacquit the holder thereof from further
liabilityto said court or county forsaid sum .

It grants the association the necessary powers to do so on equitable principles, including the power to sell and convey its property, as provided in its original incorporation act.
keywords: Louisville Pilots' Benevolent and Relief Association
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct acts. The first act (sections 4-7) deals with the financial settlement and disbursement procedures for a county treasurer and sheriff. The second act (Chapter 1268) concerns the winding up of the Louisville Pilots' Benevolent and Relief Association. Neither of these acts contains any provisions that are indicative of Jim Crow laws, such as racial segregation, discrimination, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, the classification is 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, category as null, and racial indicator as 'none'. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of E. P. Graves, of Fayette County
classification.summary: This act allows E. P. Graves of Fayette County to be licensed to practice law if he is certified by the county court as a person of honesty, probity, and good demeanor, and is examined and found qualified by two Judges of the Court of Appeals, or two Circuit Judges, or a Circuit Judge and the Chancellor of the city of Louisville. Upon being licensed, he will be authorized to practice law as if he were of the full age of twenty-one years, but will remain responsible for his contracts and duties as an attorney.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the licensing of an individual to practice law. It specifies the qualifications and examination process for E. P. Graves. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Louisville and Salt River Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Louisville and Salt River Turnpike Road Company. It acknowledges a sale of the company's assets under a court decree, the confirmation of that sale, and the subsequent transfer of ownership to new parties who have elected a managing committee. The act's primary purpose is to facilitate the continued operation and management of the turnpike road under its new ownership.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about corporate and property law, with no racial implications.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville and Portland Canal Company
classification.summary: This statute details the operational and governance requirements for the Louisville and Portland Canal Company, including the election of officers, their duties, the maintenance of the canal, and the establishment of toll rates. It specifies that officers must be stockholders and outlines the voting rights of stockholders based on their share ownership. The statute also permits the company to establish toll rates, with a specific provision allowing for 'full rates' on wagons hauling non-farm products like wood, lumber, and stone, distinguishing this from merchandise hauling.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text focuses on the corporate governance, operational duties, and financial regulations of the Louisville and Portland Canal Company. It outlines procedures for electing officers, managing the company's affairs, maintaining the canal, and setting toll rates. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also covers the requirement for gatekeepers to provide bonds for faithful performance and the procedures for reporting collected tolls.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 225 1871 .
classification.summary: This section of the 1871 Kentucky Laws outlines various powers granted to town councils, including provisions for fire prevention and extinguishment, organization of fire companies, regulation of chimneys and flues, and taking enumerations of inhabitants. It also details the regulation of town officers, elections, compensation, and fees for jurors and witnesses. Furthermore, it grants the power to regulate town police, impose fines and penalties for ordinance breaches, and enforce these penalties, with a proviso that the right of trial by jury shall not be denied. The town council is empowered to make and enforce ordinances not repugnant to the Kentucky Constitution, which must be posted within one month of passage and published before enforcement. Ordinances can be proven by the corporate seal for evidence in courts. Article VI discusses the role of the town president, including presiding over council meetings, having a casting vote, and calling special meetings. The president is also tasked with enforcing laws and ordinances, inspecting subordinate officers, and is authorized to call upon male inhabitants over twenty-one to aid in enforcement.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the general governmental powers of a town council in Kentucky in 1871. It covers a range of municipal functions such as fire safety, police regulation, and the enforcement of local ordinances. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute's focus is on the structure and powers of local governance, not on racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Madisonville
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Madisonville, Kentucky. It outlines the powers and duties of the town's police judge, town marshal, and seven trustees. It details their qualifications (white males over twenty-one), oath requirements, bond executions, and the process for assessing and collecting taxes. The trustees are empowered to maintain streets, open new ones, pay damages, advertise and let out repairs and improvements to the lowest bidder, and make by-laws for the town's government. They can also tax shows and exhibitions and employ attorneys. All fines, forfeitures, and taxes are to be paid to the chairman of the trustees for the town's benefit. The act also specifies the election process for the police judge.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the officers of the town shall consist of a police judge, town marshal, and seven trustees, all of whom 'shall be residents of said town, white males over twenty-one years old'. This explicit racial qualification for holding public office in the town makes it a Jim Crow-era law. While it doesn't directly segregate public spaces or impose differential penalties based on race, the requirement for all officers to be white is a clear indicator of racial discrimination embedded in the legal framework of the time. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the racial requirement is explicit. The category is null because the statute primarily deals with municipal governance and does not fall into the specific categories provided (education, housing, marriage, public accommodations, incarceration, voting, labor).

1871 . CHAPTER 1317 .
AN ACT regulatingthe rateof Freights and Tollson the Paris and North
Middletown Turnpike Road .
Be it enactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That the Paris and North Middletown Turnpike
Road Company shallhave power and authorityto charge
the following rate of tolls, and confine wagons to the fol-
lowing weights of freight on said road, viz: For each
wagon drawn by sixhorses, $ 1;by five,85 cents; by four,
75 cents; by three,50 cents; by two ,30 cents;by one ,20
cents; sleigh drawn by two horses, 20 cents; by one , 10
cents; barouche and other carriage drawn by two horses,
25 cents; by one ,20 cents; buggy or gig drawn by two
horses, 20 cents; by one, 10 cents; dray drawn by one
horse, 10 cents; by two ,20 cents; horse and rider,5 cents;
ledhorse,5 cents; for mules and horses in droves of ten
and under , 5 cents; over, 3 cents; asses led or driven,
same as mules ; cattle, 2 cents each ; hogs, 1 cent, and
sheep,4 cent,to be charged and collected at each gate
on their road between Paris and North Middletown : Pro-
vided,however,That wagons passingover said road shall
not haul over ten thousand pounds ,including the wagon ,
at any one load,except from the 15th day of May until
the 15th of November , inclusive, except the same be
heavy irons or castings that cannot be divided. From
the 15th of May untilthe 15th of November they are
allowed to haul twelve thousand pounds , including the
wagon .
§2 . The company may erect scales at such convenient
place or places along the line of said road as they may
designate,to determine the weight of wagons and their
contents; and may , at their pleasure, require wagons
using theirroad to be weighed with theirloads upon
such scales;and upon thefailureor refusalof any wag-
oner to weigh , as aforesaid,such ,or the owners thereof,
shallforfeitand pay the sum of ten dollarsforeach and
every failureor refusal,the same to be collectedas pro-
vided in the charterof saidcompany ; and ifa greater
load be found than is allowed in the first section of this
act,then theyshallpay to the company ,or theiragents,
the sum offiftycentsfor each one hundred pounds so
found,at the discretionof the board: Provided, however,
That no wagon shallpay for so weighing .
§3 . This act shall take effectfrom and after its pas-
sage,and shallapply to allroads in which the Bourbon
countycourtowns stock,unlessotherwiseorderedby the
board of directorsof said turnpike roads.
Approved February 17, 1871.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act creating a Sinking Fund for Montgomery County," passed March 10th, 1856.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the Sinking Fund for Montgomery County. It specifies that no more than fifty cents per hundred dollars shall be levied for the fund, and this money is to be used solely for the payment of the principal and interest of railroad bonds issued by the county to the Lexington and Big Sandy Railroad. All other provisions of the original act remain in effect unless in conflict with this amendment.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to an act concerning a sinking fund for Montgomery County, specifically related to the payment of railroad bonds. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high as the content is clearly financial and administrative, with no racial implications.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Trustees of the Town of Sebree City
classification.summary: This statute incorporates the "Trustees of the Town of Sebree City" as a body politic and corporate. It outlines the powers and duties of the trustees, including electing a chairman and clerk, receiving and disbursing town funds, making annual tax assessments (not exceeding fifty cents on each one hundred dollars of property and a poll-tax not exceeding two dollars), maintaining streets and sidewalks, opening new streets, making by-laws for the town's government, taxing shows and exhibitions, and employing attorneys. It also empowers the trustees to appoint a police judge and marshal.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and governance of the town of Sebree City. It details the administrative and financial powers of the town's trustees. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes the Germantown and Bridgeville Turnpike Road Company to erect a toll-gate within one mile of Germantown and to collect tolls. It also designates an area within half a mile of the road on either side as a road district, where residents liable for road taxes or work must contribute to this specific road, with the collected taxes accruing to the company for road repair.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details an act for the benefit of a turnpike road company, authorizing toll collection and the use of local road taxes for road maintenance. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that suggest differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high as the content is clearly unrelated to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the support of paupers in Mercer county.
classification.summary: This act establishes a superintendent for paupers in Mercer County, outlining their duties in providing maintenance, support, and burial for individuals deemed paupers. It specifically mentions a 'Negro Pauper Fund' that must be exhausted before other funds are used for the benefit of 'negro paupers'. The act defines who qualifies as a pauper, including colored persons who are residents, lack property, are incapable of labor, and whose parents cannot support them.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions a 'Negro Pauper Fund' and defines eligibility for pauper support to include 'colored persons'. This direct reference to race in the context of public assistance and defining who is a charge on the county strongly indicates a Jim Crow-era law. The law creates a separate fund and potentially separate treatment for 'negro paupers', which aligns with the discriminatory practices of the Jim Crow era. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1871. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language and the context of public welfare, which was often a site of racial discrimination during this period. The category is 'other' as it doesn't fit neatly into the provided categories like education, housing, etc., but relates to public welfare and social services.

The bonds will bear 10% annual interest, have a 10-year term, and be redeemable after two years. The corporate committee will determine bond denominations, prescribe their form, and appoint a president and secretary. A sinking fund will be created from lodge revenues to cover interest and principal payments. The lodge's property will be bound for bond payments.

It specifies the road's path from Adair County to Greensburg and then to Larue County, with a provision to prioritize routes where citizens and property owners offer the most liberal contributions and stock subscriptions. The board is authorized to occupy public roads as needed for construction.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize the County Court of Green County to Subscribe to the Capital Stock of the Greensburg, Columbia, and New Haven Turnpike Road Company, and to Issue Bonds for the Payment Thereof.
classification.summary: This statute authorizes the Green County Court to issue bonds to subscribe to the capital stock of the Greensburg, Columbia, and New Haven Turnpike Road Company. The bonds are to be in denominations of $100 to $1000, payable in 10, 20, or 30 years, with interest at 6% or 8% per annum. The county court is to levy an ad valorem tax on real and personal estate to pay the interest on these bonds. The sheriff will collect this tax and pay it to the company's treasurer or a special commissioner.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial authorization for a turnpike road company and the mechanism for funding it through county bonds and taxation. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville, and for other purposes.
classification.summary: This act contains several provisions related to the city of Louisville's finances and operations. Section 9 allows the sinking fund commissioners to purchase stock in the Louisville Water Company. Section 10 modifies publication requirements for city council proceedings, mandating publication in two newspapers, one English and one German, with specified rate limits. Section 11 authorizes the issuance of city bonds for various purposes, including paying old liabilities, raising street grades, building a sewer, and constructing a roadbed for a railroad.

classification.title: An Act to Constitute a District for the Completion of a Turnpike Road
classification.summary: This act establishes a specific district for the purpose of aiding in the completion of a turnpike road. It defines the boundaries of this district and appoints appraisers to assess the value of land within the district as it will be enhanced by the road's completion. The appraisers are to report their findings to the turnpike company.

It details the geographical boundaries of this district and the process for appraising land values within it to contribute to the road's construction. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Pond Creek Turnpike Road Company," approved March 10, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the Pond Creek Turnpike Road Company. It details the process for collecting taxes assessed against individuals for the turnpike, including tax bills, constable collection, commissions, and potential lawsuits. Crucially, it mandates that the tax will only be levied after a vote by qualified voters, including all landowners within the district, at an election held on the first Saturday in May 1871 at the Pond Creek schoolhouse. The election is to be conducted by appointed officials who will open a poll book and record votes for and against the tax.

This combined tax will form a sinking fund exclusively for paying the principal and interest on city bonds. The levy will be regulated by financial statements and assessor's books, ensuring no more tax is levied than necessary. If the full levy is insufficient, funds will be drawn from the general tax. The statute also details penalties for delinquent taxes, including a twenty percent penalty for the first return of delinquency, which can be carried forward annually. It establishes a lien on property for unpaid taxes, penalties, and costs, and outlines the city's right to enforce this lien through equity suits and distress for collection.

It outlines the judge's oath, jurisdiction (civil and criminal, similar to justices of the peace, but excluding courts of inquiry), authority over town ordinances, record-keeping, power to summon witnesses and compel attendance, power to fine and imprison for contempt (with limits), and the ability to order juries. classification.reasoning: The statute establishes the judicial powers and procedures for a Police Judge in the town of Casey's Mines. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a standard piece of local governance legislation establishing a judicial office. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to authorize the sale of the grounds and buildings of St. James (late Shelby) College, and to establish a graded public school in Shelbyville.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the board of trustees of Shelbyville, Kentucky, to sell the grounds and buildings of St. James (late Shelby) College, except for the college building and at least two acres adjoining it. The proceeds from the sale are to be used for repairing the college building and grounds for a graded public school, with any remaining funds invested for the school's maintenance. A common school district is to be laid off, and five trustees elected to manage the school's funds and operations.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Abbott's Landing and Big Twin Creek Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Abbott's Landing and Big Twin Creek Turnpike Road Company. It outlines the appointment of commissioners, the subscription of stock, the election of a president and directors, and their powers. It also specifies the road's construction standards, toll collection, and the acquisition of right-of-way. Eligibility for office requires stock ownership. The act details the process for appointing and removing other officers and agents, and the requirement for bonds. Subscribers must agree to pay for their shares as required by the company. The road must be at least thirty feet wide, with a grade width of at least twenty feet and a metal width of at least twelve feet, with an elevation not exceeding five degrees. A toll-gate can be erected once five continuous miles are completed.
